dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My daughter has been up all night. She has facial swelling and a lot of eye pain.
Has she had other symptoms like difficulty breathing, a rash, or anything else?
She has some trouble breathing, and her nose hurts. I don't see any rash on her.
Sophia, can you tell me your name and age?
My name is Sophia, and I'm 7 years old.
She has a patent airway, and her voice sounds normal without change.
Sophia, when did the swelling and pain start for you?
About 30 minutes ago. She woke me up saying her eyes hurt.
She has wheezing bilaterally and her breathing rate is within normal limits.
Her work of breathing looks normal with no signs of acute distress.
Sophia, we're going to take your pulse now. Just stay still.
Her pulse is 97 beats per minute and is strong and regular.
Her respiratory rate is 20, and she's breathing at a normal rate.
Sophia, we're going to check your blood pressure now. It is 98/0, which is within normal limits.
Her oxygen saturation is 98% on room air.
Her blood glucose is currently not available. She appears alert and not hypoglycemic.
We've placed a four-lead ECG monitor for cardiac rhythm. Her rhythm is sinus rhythm.
Sophia, how would you describe how you're feeling right now?
I'd say I'm okay. It's not too bad.
She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. She is ambulatory and not in acute distress.
She is conscious, responds verbally, and her pupils are equal and reactive to light. No signs of fever noted.
She has no signs of trauma, warm and dry skin, and no rash observed. No fever is present.
Eyes are swollen and tearing. Skin is appropriate color without rashes or hives noted.
Placing a four-lead ECG now for baseline rhythm as part of the general examination protocol.
Does she have any allergies or a history of previous allergic reactions?
She has no known drug allergies and no previous allergic reactions.
Administering 35mg of Benadryl IM to treat the allergic reaction now. The injection is given in the left deltoid.
Repeating the four-lead ECG for rhythm reassessment. Persistent sinus rhythm noted.
Sophia, we'll move you to the stretcher now. Do you think you can walk?
She is able to walk on her own. Let's help her up.
She is now secured on the stretcher with all safety straps in place. She remains alert and talkative during transport preparation.
I would like her to go to [COMPANY_NAME]; she has a family connection there.
We are calling in that we're transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] for allergy-related symptoms. Protocol followed is medical - allergic reaction/anaphylaxis (protocol 9-2).
Standard protocols will be followed for moving a patient in her condition. She shows no signs of anaphylaxis beyond facial swelling and wheezing.
She denies any nausea or abdominal pain and is coherent and clear-headed during our examination.
Bystander states the patient ate crabs the previous night with no initial reaction reported until this morning.
We are preparing for transport and will start shortly. Oxygen is administered at 1L NC as per protocol.
Sophia is sitting up comfortably and can walk to the stretcher without assistance as noted in the narrative. No need for apheresis noted in this case.
She is stable and ready for transport. COVID-19 precautions will be observed during transport as specified in the narrative.
We're heading to [COMPANY_NAME].
